**Relevance:** The CDBR (now part of prestigious Man and Biosphere programme of UNESCO) is India’s first high altitude Cold desert biosphere reserve that has been included in World network of biosphere reserves by UNESCO on 27 Sep, 2025.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Transport (Roadways, Railways, Waterways, Ports, Airways, Pipelines, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with respect to
ports in India:
I. 	 Maritime State Development Council is a
consultative body to coordinate between the Centre and coastal States on the matters of port development.
II.	 Only major ports are operated under the
administrative purview of the Union Ministry of Ports, Shipping and Waterways.
III. 	State Maritime Boards are statutory bodies
established by coastal States  to manage nonmajor ports.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **The Indian Ports Act**, 2025 (which replaced the Indian Ports Act, 1908) grants statutory status to the **Maritime State Development Council (MSDC)**. The purpose of the MSDC is **to serve as a consultative** **body** for **coordinating** between the **Central and coastal State** Governments on matters of port development and policy. **So, statement I is correct.**
● India hosts 12 major ports and 200+ non-major (minor) ports across its coastline. **The major ports** **operate under the administrative purview of the Ministry of Shipping,** while non-major ports fall under the jurisdiction of respective State Maritime Boards or State Governments. **So, statement II is** **correct.**
● **State Maritime Boards** are recognised as a statutory body under the 2025 Act, which would be established by coastal states and empowered **to manage non-major ports**. These Boards will oversee port planning, infrastructure development, licensing, tariff regulation, and enforcement of safety, security, and environmental compliances. **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**Key Features of the Indian Ports Act, 2025**
● **Port officers**: The Act designates the conservator - appointed by the government - as the port officer, with authority over other officers.
o The conservator retains powers over vessel movement within port limits and fee recovery, and adds new responsibilities for disease control, damage assessment, and penalty adjudication.
● **Dispute Resolution:** State governments must set up **Dispute Resolution** Committees (DRCs) to resolve conflicts among non-major ports, concessionaires, users, and service providers. Appeals go to the High Court, not civil courts.
● **Tariffs:** At major ports it will be determined either by the Board of the Major Port Authority or by the Board of Directors if the port operates as a registered company.
o **For non-major ports,** tariff-setting authority rests with the State Maritime Board or its designated concessionaire. o Further, the Indian Port Act 2025 requires tariffs and charges to be published
electronically for transparency.
● **Safety provisions:** The Act upholds penalties for safety violations like damaging buoys or mishandling combustibles on board.
o It strengthens environmental protections by aligning with global conventions
(MARPOL, Ballast Water Management) and introducing new mandates for pollution control and disaster readiness.
**Relevance**: Parliament passed the Indian Ports Act, 2025 to consolidate law relating to ports, facilitating ease of doing business.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.questionText:
With reference to the CAG’s report on public debt,
consider the following statements:
I. 	 The combined public debt of 28 States has
tripled over a decade between 2013-14 to 2022-23.
II. 	 Punjab recorded the highest debt-to-GSDP
ratio.
III. 	Eight states had public debt liability of more
than 30 per cent of their GSDP.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● According to the Comptroller and Auditor General of India (CAG), **the combined public debt (internal** **debt and loans & advances from the Centre) of 28 States tripled over a decade**, rising from Rs **17.57** **lakh crore in 2013-14 to Rs 59.60 lakh crore in 2022-23**. In terms of **Gross State Domestic Product** **(GSDP),** the debt increased from 16.66% of GSDP in 2013-14 to **22.96% in 2022-23. So, statement I is** **correct.**
○ **GSDP** represents the total value of all finished goods and services produced within a State. ○ **Debt** is measured in terms of **internal debt** and **loans & advances from the Centre**.
● According to the report, at the end of 2022-23, the **highest debt-to-GSDP ratio of 40.35 per cent** was recorded in **Punjab, followed by Nagaland (37.15 per cent) and West Bengal (33.70 per cent).** The lowest ratios were recorded in Odisha (8.45 per cent), Maharashtra (14.64 per cent) and Gujarat (16.37 per cent). **So, statement II is correct.**
● As on 31st March 2023, **eight states had public debt liability of more than 30 per cent of their GSDP;** six states had public debt liability of less than 20 per cent of their GSDP and remaining 14 states had public debt liability between 20 to 30 per cent of their respective GSDP in FY 2022-23, the report said. **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** The recent CAG report on the fiscal health of States highlights a sharp increase in public debt and its implications for fiscal sustainability.

questions.subject: Environment
questions.microTopic: Air, Water, Noise Pollution, SLCPs, etc.
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. 	 Biological Oxygen Demand (BOD) indicates
the level of organic pollution in the water bodies.
II. 	 As per recent Central Pollution Control
Board assessment, there has been an increase in pollution level in Indian rivers.
III. 	Punjab recorded the highest number of
polluted river stretches.
Which of the statements given above are **not** correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB)** monitors the health of India’s rivers using a parameter called **Biological Oxygen Demand (BOD). BOD measures the amount of dissolved organic matter in** **water**, with **higher BOD values indicating greater organic pollution**. Rivers with BOD exceeding **3** **mg/L are considered unfit for bathing**. **So, statement I is correct.**
● According to the **2023 CPCB assessment,** the number of river locations deemed unfit for bathing **decreased from 815 in 2022 to 807 in 2023,** indicating a **slight improvement.** **So, statement II is not** **correct.**
○ Polluted River Stretches (PRS) – 296 PRS across 271 rivers (down from 311 across 279 rivers in 2022). ○ State-wise – Maharashtra (54) highest PRS, followed by Kerala (31), MP & Manipur (18 each), Karnataka (14).
● **Maharashtra recorded the highest number of polluted PRS (54**), but **Tamil Nadu, Uttar Pradesh** **and Uttarakhand** each had the **highest number of Priority 1 stretches** (most severely polluted river stretches), requiring immediate attention. **So, statement III is not correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** According to the recent report by CPCB, Tamil Nadu, Uttar Pradesh and Uttarakhand had the largest number of polluted sites.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. 	 The Sawalkote Hydro Power Project is a run-
of-the-river hydroelectric plant proposed on the Chenab River in Jammu and Kashmir.
II. 	 The Oju Hydroelectric Project is a proposed
project on the Subansiri River in Arunachal Pradesh.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Sawalkote Hydropower Project** is a 2,185 MW **run-of-the-river hydroelectric plant** proposed on the **Chenab River** in the Ramban district of **Jammu and Kashmir.** It is set to become the largest hydropower project in the Union Territory and one of the biggest in North India. It will be implemented by **NHPC Ltd** in collaboration with the **Jammu and Kashmir State Power Development Corporation** **Limited (JKSPDC)** at an estimated cost of ₹22,704 crore. **So, statement I is correct.**
● The **Oju Hydroelectric Project** is proposed on the **Subansiri River** and it will be developed by **Oju** **Subansiri Hydro Power Corporation Pvt Ltd.** The main power plant will have a capacity of 2,100 MW, while the dam-toe plant will have a capacity of 120MW. It is located uppermost among a cascade of hydroelectric projects planned in the Subansiri basin, and will be the largest in terms of its approved capacity. **So, statement II is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Months after suspending the Indus Waters Treaty, India is reviving the long-delayed Sawalkote Hydropower Project on the Chenab.

questions.subject: Environment
questions.questionText:
With reference to the 'Environmental Accounting
on Forest 2025' report, consider the following statements:
I. 	 It is released annually by the Ministry of
Environment, Forest and Climate Change in collaboration with the Food and Agriculture Organization.
II. 	 It assesses the economic value of 'Regulating
Services' like carbon retention in monetary terms.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
The **Ministry of Statistics and Programme Implementation (MoSPI)** adopted the UN **System of** **Environmental Economic Accounts (SEEA) Framework** in 2018 which is an agreed international framework for the compilation of the Environment Economic accounts since.
● In 2025, the Ministry of Statistics and Programme Implementation (MoSPI) compiled and released the 8th consecutive issue of the publication related to environment accounts titled **“Environmental** **Accounting on forest -2025**.
o **This is the first dedicated publication on forest accounting** compiled with the objective of presenting comprehensive information on forest accounts at both the national and state levels, in accordance with the SEEA framework. The publication is compiled in two separate volumes. **So,** **statement I is not correct.**
● The report's primary function is Environmental-Economic Accounting. It assesses the economic value of 'Provisioning Services' (like timber and non-timber forest produce (NTFP)) and **'Regulating Services'** **(like carbon retention)** in monetary terms. **So, statement II is correct.**
o According to this report the forest service account of India shows that **provisioning services** **(timber and non-timber products)** increased from ₹30.72 to ₹37.93 thousand crore between 2011-12 and 2021-22, about 0.16% of GDP at current price in 2021-22. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**Key Outcome of Environmental Accounting on forest -2025 Report**
● **Physical Asset account:** From 2010-11 to 2021-22, India’s forest cover grew by 17,444.61 sq. km (22.50%), reaching 7.15 lakh sq. km (21.76% of geographical area), indicating effective regeneration and conservation efforts.
o Major state-wise increases: Kerala (4,137 sq. km), Karnataka (3,122 sq. km), and Tamil Nadu (2,606 sq. km).
● **Condition Account**: Growing stock, a key indicator in the forest condition account, reflects the volume of usable wood in living trees. Between 2013 and 2023, India’s growing stock rose by 305.53 million cum (7.32%) during 2013-2023.
o Biggest contributors: Madhya Pradesh (136 million cum), Chhattisgarh (51
million cum), and Telangana (28 million cum); among UTs, Andaman & Nicobar Islands (77 million cum).
● **The forest service account** of India reports a 51.82% rise in carbon retention regulating services, from ₹409.1 to ₹620.97 thousand crore between 2015-16 and 2021-22, emphasizing forests’ vital role in climate change mitigation and national goals.
o The value in 2021-22 is equivalent to 2.63% of GDP at current price. Leading contributors: Arunachal Pradesh (296), Uttarakhand (156.6), and Assam (129.96) thousand crore.
**Relevance**: Ministry of Statistics and Programme Implementation (MoSPI) released the 8th consecutive issue of the publication related to environment accounts titled “Environmental Accounting on forest -2025.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.questionText: A quasi-satellite of the Earth is :

options.a:
An asteroid that orbits the Sun in a similar
path to Earth without being gravitationally bound by the Earth.
options.b:
An Earth-like small asteroid in the asteroid-
belt between Mars and Jupiter.
options.c:
An asteroid that orbits the Moon without
being gravitationally bound by the Moon.
options.d:
An asteroid that orbits the Earth in a similar
path to the Moon without being gravitationally bound by the Earth.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **A quasi-satellite is a celestial object, often an asteroid, that orbits the Sun while remaining in close** **proximity to a planet (**without being gravitationally bound by the Earth**).** It shares the same orbital period as the planet, meaning both complete their orbit around the Sun in the same amount of time.
● However, the quasi-satellite's orbit is typically more eccentric, resembling a horseshoe shape, and it crosses the planet's orbital path. Notably, many planets in the Solar System, including Earth, have quasisatellites.
● Earth is home to over twenty recognized quasi-satellites, with 2016 HO3 (also known as 469219 Kamo'oalewa) being the most studied. This particular quasi-satellite is notable for its size, distance from Earth, and the hypothesis that it may be a fragment of the Moon. Quasi-satellites are integral to understanding celestial dynamics, as they interact gravitationally with their nearby planets while being primarily influenced by the Sun. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Recently, astronomers have discovered Arjuna 2025 PN7, a new quasi-satellite of Earth, which was first detected by the Pan-STARRS 1 telescope in Hawaii.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. 	 ‘Extreme nuclear transients’ occur when
supermassive black holes tear apart stars, releasing massive amounts of energy.
II. 	 ‘Gamma-ray bursts’ are caused by the
collapse of a massive star that gives birth to a black hole.
III. 	‘Tidal disruption events’ take place when a
star is pulled apart by the tidal forces of a black hole.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Extreme Nuclear Transients (ENTs)** are the most powerful energy blasts known in the universe. These transients are objects whose brightness changes rapidly and are fueled by material from a massive star torn apart by a supermassive black hole. ENTs occur when **supermassive black holes tear apart stars,** **releasing massive amounts of energy**. ENTs can emit 100 times more energy in a single year than the Sun produces over its entire 10-billion-year lifetime. **So, statement I is correct.**
● **Gamma-ray bursts (GRBs)** are among the most powerful and violent explosions in the known universe. The cause of a gamma-ray burst depends on how long it lasts.
○ GRBs that last less than two seconds are caused by the merger of two neutron stars or the merger of a neutron star and a black hole. ○ Longer GRBs, which can last hours, are triggered **when a massive star collapses and births a** **black hole.** **So, statement II is correct.** ○ In both cases, scientists think GRBs result from jets of particles accelerated to around 99.9% the speed of light.
● **Tidal disruption events (TDEs)** are astronomical phenomena that **occur when a star passes close** **enough to a supermassive black hole and is pulled apart by the black hole’s tidal forces**, causing the process of disruption. **So, statement III is correct.**
○ Such tidally disrupted stellar debris starts raining down on the black hole and radiation emerges from the innermost region of accreting debris, which is an indicator of the presence of a TDE. **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Astronomers have recently discovered a new class of cosmic phenomena called Extreme Nuclear Transients (ENTs), which are even more energetic than Gamma-Ray Bursts (GRBs).

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.microTopic: Reports and Indices (HDI, CPI, GHI, WEO, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Which one of the following bodies publishes the
annual Global Innovation Index (GII)?

options.d:
International Association for the Protection
of Intellectual Property

questions.explanationMarkdown:
The **Global Innovation Index (GII) 2025** ranks the world's most innovative economies and identifies the **top** **100** **innovation** **clusters globally.** It highlights global innovation trends based on investment patterns, technological progress, adoption rates and socioeconomic impacts.
● It is produced by  the **World Intellectual Property Organisation (WIPO).** As per the Index, R&D growth fell to 2.9% in 2024 and is projected to drop further to 2.3% in 2025.
● China has entered the **top 10 of the Global Innovation Index 2025** for the first time, securing the **10th** **position** and emerging as the **leading middle-income economy**.
● Meanwhile, **India has climbed to 38th place among 139 economies**, marking a **notable rise from its** **81st rank in 2015**. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** India has been ranked 38th globally out of 139 economies in the WIPO Global Innovation Index (GII) 2025.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to
the Child Nutrition Report, 2025:
I. 	 The report is published by the NGO Children’s
Foundation.
II. 	 Children constitute the largest proportion of
those living with overweight in the world.
III. 	The Food Safety and Standards Authority of
India has set a 2% limit on trans fats in all food products by total fat content.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **UNICEF (United Nations Children's Fund) recently produced the Child Nutrition Report, 2025.** It was titled ‘Feeding Profit: How Food Environments Are Failing Children’ and estimates that one in five children and adolescents aged 5 to 19 years is living with overweight. **So, statement I is not correct.**
○ **Children's Foundation was started by Kailash Satyarthi**, which advocates for child labor prevention and education.
● **Children aged 5–9 years and 10–14 years represent the highest proportions of those living with** **overweight.**  **So, statement II is correct.**
● In 2021, India became the first lower-middle-income country to adopt the World Health Organisation (WHO) best-practice policies to limit trans fats. The Food Safety and Standards Authority of India (FSSAI) set a **2% limit on trans fats in all food products** by total fat content, effective from 2022. **So,** **statement III is correct.**
○ This regulation aims to eliminate industrially produced trans fats, a major dietary risk factor for heart disease, and forms part of a global initiative to safeguard public health from harmful fats. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** A recent UNICEF report estimates that one in five children and adolescents between 5 and 19 years is ‘living with overweight’.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.questionText:
The IS4OM Centre and Project NETRA are
primarily associated with:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
After a near-collision in 2024 between an Indian satellite and a foreign spacecraft, the Government of India is planning to deploy **“bodyguard satellites”** to protect its space assets. This initiative is expected to form part of a broader strategy incorporating new surveillance satellites, radars, telescopes and advanced technologies such as **LiDAR for enhanced space situational awareness.**
● In India, the **Indian Space Situational Awareness and Management (IS4OM) Centre in Bengaluru** functions as a **satellite-tracking hub,** **monitoring satellites**, issuing warnings for potential collisions and **coordinating avoidance manoeuvres**. In 2023 alone, Indian satellites carried out more than 10 collisionavoidance manoeuvres.
● India’s **Project NETRA** is expanding these capabilities through new radars and telescopes, including the **Multi-Object Tracking Radar at Sriharikota,** with additional sites planned nationwide. However, India does not yet have the capability for continuous monitoring. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** India is planning to deploy “bodyguard satellites” alongside multiple protective measures to secure its space assets.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.questionText:
India's first overseas defence manufacturing
facility has been established in which one of the following countries?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Defence Minister of India Rajnath Singh and his Moroccan counterpart, Abdelatif Loudyi recently jointly inaugurated **Tata Advanced Systems Limited’s (TASL)** **defence manufacturing facility** in **Berrechid,** **Morocco.** The facility will undertake the production of an indigenously developed **Wheeled Armoured** **Platform**, jointly designed by TASL and the **Defence Research and Development Organisation** **(DRDO)**.
○ The TASL will deliver these vehicles to the Royal Moroccan Army, with initial deliveries scheduled to begin next month. This is the Kingdom of Morocco’s largest defence manufacturing facility, marking **the first such plant by an Indian private company overseas.**
● The **Wheeled Armoured Platform** is a **modular combat platform** with advanced protection, mobility and adaptability. Configurations include infantry fighting vehicle, armoured personnel carrier, reconnaissance, command post, mortar carrier and ambulance variants, with options for remote weapon stations and anti-tank guided missile systems. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** The Defence Minister of India recently inaugurated India’s first overseas defence facility in Morocco.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.questionText:
With reference to the India’s nuclear fusion power
roadmap, consider the following statements:
I. 	 SST-Bharat is India’s first fusion-based
power generator.
II. 	 Superconducting magnets are used in fusion
reactors to contain superheated plasma.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **SST-Bharat (Steady-State Superconducting Tokamak-Bharat):** It is planned as **India’s first fusion-** **based power generator** and will operate as a fusion–fission hybrid reactor. **So, statement I is correct.**
○ It aims to produce 130 MW of electricity-30 MW from fusion and 100 MW from fission-by using fusion neutrons to drive a thorium-based subcritical fission system. ○ This design marks a major step toward achieving full fusion energy while advancing fuel breeding and reducing nuclear waste.
● **Superconducting magnets:** They are key to both the SST-1 and the proposed SST-Bharat, as they create powerful magnetic fields to confine plasma at temperatures over 100 million °C within a toroidal chamber. Superconducting magnets are essential for **magnetic confinement fusion reactors, like tokamaks, to** **contain superheated plasma.** SST-1, India’s first tokamak with such magnets, enabled long-pulse operations of up to 1000 seconds. Building on this, SST-Bharat aims for continuous plasma operation, achievable only with advanced superconducting magnet technology. **So, statement II is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Environment
questions.microTopic: Greenhouse Gases (GHG), ODS, Related Terms, Global Warming
questions.questionText:
The Production Gap Report is related to which
one of the following?

options.b:
Fossil fuel production and climate change
targets
options.d:
Global trade deficits and export-import
imbalance

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Production Gap Report** tracks the discrepancy between governments' planned fossil fuel production (coal, oil, and gas) and the production levels that are consistent with global climate targets, specifically the goals set in the Paris Agreement to limit global warming to 1.5°C or 2°C. This "gap" highlights the difference between what is being produced or planned to be produced and what is needed to avoid severe climate change impacts.
● It is released by a collaboration of leading research organizations, including the Stockholm Environment Institute (SEI), Climate Analytics, and the International Institute for Sustainable Development (IISD), often with support from the United Nations Environment Programme (UNEP). **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Environment
questions.questionText:
Which one of the following country/organisation
has launched the Tropical Forests Forever Facility?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Tropical Forests Forever Facility** is an innovative multilateral global permanent fund dedicated to supporting tropical forest conservation over the long-term.
● **It is a global initiative led by the Government of Brazil.**
● The idea for the fund was presented by the Brazilian government in 2023, at COP28 in the United Arab Emirates.
● Goal: The TFFF is a blended finance structure that seeks to mobilize US$125 billion in capital from public and private sector sources.
● The fund would invest the money into a diversified portfolio that generates a return that is higher than its cost of capital.
● This would be used to pay the tropical forest countries (TFC) a fixed amount of money per hectare of standing forest. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Brazil will become the first country to announce an investment in the Tropical Forests Forever Facility.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part III, IV & IVA: Rights and Duties
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. 	 The Right to reputation is a Fundamental
Right under the Constitution of India.
II. 	 Defamation is a reasonable restriction on the
freedom of speech and expression in India.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The Supreme Court has consistently held that the Right to reputation is a fundamental component of the right to life and personal liberty guaranteed under Article 21 of the Constitution. In the landmark case of **Subramanian Swamy v. Union of India (2016)**, the Court explicitly stated that “a **good reputation** is an element of personal security and is protected by the Constitution”, making it an **inseparable facet of** **Article 21**. The Court emphasized that reputation is not just a social value but a constitutional right, essential to living with dignity. **So, statement I is correct.**
● While Article 19(1)(a) guarantees the freedom of speech and expression, it is not absolute. Under Article 19(2), **the State can impose reasonable restrictions in the interests** of public order, decency, morality, and **defamation**. In the Subramanian Swamy case, the Supreme Court upheld the validity of Sections 499 and 500 of the IPC, ruling that protecting an individual’s reputation is a legitimate ground for limiting free speech through criminal defamation. **So, statement II is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Recently, an SC Judge highlighted the need to decriminalise defamation.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Marine Resources, Maritime Zones, UNCLOS, ISA, etc.
questions.questionText:
With reference to International Seabed Authority
(ISA), consider the following statements:
I. 	 It was established under the United Nations
Convention on the Law of the Sea.
II. 	 It regulates all mineral-related activities in
the international seabed area only within Exclusive Economic Zone.
III. 	India is the only country to hold two
Polymetallic Sulphides exploration contracts with the ISA.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **International Seabed Authority (ISA) is an independent organization** created **under** the Part XI of the **United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea (UNCLOS**). **So, statement I is correct.**
● It is responsible for organizing, regulating, and controlling **all mineral-related activities in the** **international seabed area beyond the limits of national jurisdiction. Its jurisdiction extends even** **beyond Exclusive Economic Zone and includes all High Seas areas.** Its main goal is to ensure that these resources are used for the benefit of all humankind, while promoting environmental protection, scientific research, and fair sharing of benefits. **So, statement II is not correct.**
● As of September 2025, **India is the first and only country to hold two exploration contracts with the** **International Seabed Authority for Polymetallic Sulphides**. The first, signed in 2016, covers areas in the Central and Southwest Indian Ridges, while the second, signed in 2025, covers a 10,000 sq km block in the Carlsberg Ridge. **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** India hosts the 8th Annual Contractors’ Meeting of the ISA in Goa.

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Aircraft Origin
I. - Tejas Mk1A Indian
II. - Mig-21 French
III. - SEPECAT Jaguar Soviet
IV. - Sukhoi Su-30MKI Russian
How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer: d** **Explanation**:
● The **Tejas Mk1A** variant is **indigenously built fighter aircraft**, fitted with an Advanced Electronic Warfare (EW) suite with self-protection jammer and has Beyond Visual Range (BVR) missile capability (like Astra/ASRAAM integration). **So, pair I is correctly matched.**
● **The Mikoyan-Gurevich MiG-21** is an iconic **Soviet-era jet fighter** known for its simple, rugged design and supersonic speed. The most recent ‘Bison’ (BIS) variant boasts modern avionics and radar, and advanced missiles. On September 26, MiG-21 fighter jets retired in Chandigarh during a decommissioning ceremony By the Indian Air Force (IAF). **So, pair II is not correctly matched.**
● The **SEPECAT Jaguar** is a well-known **Anglo-French twin-engine jet** designed specifically for the **deep penetration strike and ground attack** (bomber/strike) role, often called the "Deep Penetration Strike Aircraft" (DPSA) in IAF service. **So, pair III is not correctly matched.**
● The **Su-30MKI,** a **Russian origin** aircraft, is currently the backbone of India's fighter aircraft inventory and **license-produced by Hindustan Aeronautics Limited (HAL),** is slated for a significant enhancement package dubbed the "Super Sukhoi" program.
o A key feature of this upgrade is the introduction of the Virupaksha radar. This advanced Active Electronically Scanned Array (AESA) radar, developed domestically, utilizes Gallium Nitride (GaN) technology and boasts around 2,400 Transmit/Receive Modules (TRMs).  **So, pair IV is** **correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.** **Relevance**: The Indian Air Force (IAF) on September 26 bid farewell to its iconic MiG-21 fighter jets.

questions.subject: Environment
questions.microTopic: Major Species (Keystone, Indicator, Invasive, Endemic, etc.)
questions.questionText:
A new plant species named Ophiorrhiza echinata,
recently in the news, is a species of
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**About Ophiorrhiza echinata**
● It is a new species of **coffee plant**.
● It was discovered in the highly biodiversity-rich shola forests of the Western Ghats at Devikulam in the Idukki District of Kerala.
● It grows in the ecotone region between evergreen forest and grassland vegetation at an elevation of 1,630 m above sea level and may have medicinal potential.
● It belongs to the **family Rubiaceae** and is closely related to the genus Ophiorrhiza mungos, which is a **key ingredient in cancer treatment and antidote preparations.**
● Ophiorrhiza echinata has so far only been collected from the type locality.
● The area of occupancy is estimated to be less than four square kilometres, and the known populations contain a maximum of only 35 plants. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** A new coffee plant species named Ophiorrhiza echinata was recently found in the Western Ghats.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with respect to
the Goods and Services Tax Appellate Tribunal (GSTAT):
I. 	 It is a constitutional body established under
Article 279A of the Constitution of India.
II. 	 It serves as the appellate authority for
resolving disputes under the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **The Goods and Services Tax Appellate Tribunal (GSTAT)** is not a constitutional body. It is **a statutory** **body established under Section 109 of the Central Goods and Services Tax Act, 2017,** not under Article 279A of the Constitution. **So, statement I is not correct.**
○ While Article 279A provides for the creation of the GST Council, which makes recommendations on various aspects of GST, it does not empower the creation of GSTAT.
● The GST Appellate Tribunal (GSTAT) is the second level of appeal under the GST dispute resolution system. **It hears cases against orders issued by Appellate and Revisional Authorities under the CGST** **Act, 2017,** and related State GST laws. Designed as a centralized forum, it ensures uniformity and fairness for taxpayers in challenging GST decisions across the country. **So, statement II is correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** Recently, Union Finance Minister launched the Goods and Services Tax Appellate Tribunal (GSTAT) in New Delhi

questions.subject: Current Affairs
questions.microTopic: Current Affairs Focus (Summits, Reports, Conflicts, Visits, etc.)
questions.questionText:
With reference to the World Food India 2025,
consider the following statements:
I. 	 The event aims to highlight nutritional
insecurity in India.
II. 	 It is an initiative of the Ministry of Agriculture
and Farmers’ Welfare.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **World Food India 2025 is India’s flagship food processing event**. **It aims to position India as a** **‘Global Food Hub’. So, statement I is not correct.**
● It was conceptualized by the **Ministry of Food Processing Industries (MoFPI).**  **So, statement II is not** **correct.**
● It has grown to showcase India as the “Food Basket of the World,” promoting investment, enhancing farm-to-fork linkages, encouraging sustainable food systems, and highlighting India’s diverse food culture.
○ The event concluded with the signing of Memoranda of Understanding (MoUs) worth over Rs 1 lakh crore,highlighting investments, innovations, and employment generation across multiple states. **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** The 4th edition of the World Food India concluded recently.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part IX, IXA, IXB: Local Governments
questions.microTopic: Local Government Panchayats, Municipalities, Cooperative Societies
questions.questionText:
Consider the following digital reforms:
I. 	 SabhaSaar
II. 	 SVAMITVA
III. 	BharatNet
IV. 	 E-Gram Swaraj
How many of the above initiatives aim to strengthen digital governance in Gram Panchayats?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **SabhaSaar is a digital initiative launched by the Ministry of Panchayati Raj to record and document** **the proceedings of Gram Sabha meetings.** By making these records accessible online, it promotes transparency, strengthens digital governance at the Gram Panchayat level, and enhances accountability and citizen participation. **So, point I is correct.**
● **SVAMITVA** (Survey of Villages and Mapping with Improvised Technology in Village Areas) scheme, launched in 2020, **uses drones to map residential land in rural areas and issue property cards to** **villagers**. This empowers **Gram Panchayats with accurate land records, enabling better property tax** **collection, dispute resolution, and planning**. The scheme directly strengthens rural governance by digitizing land ownership at the village level. **So, point II is correct.**
● **BharatNet**, one of the biggest **rural telecom projects** in the world, implemented in a phased manner to all Gram Panchayats (approximately 2.5 lakh) in the country for providing non-discriminatory access to broadband connectivity to all the telecom service providers. The **objective is to enable internet access** **providers like mobile operators, Internet Service Providers (ISPs), Cable TV operators, content** **providers** to launch various services such as applications like e-health, e-education and **e-governance in** **rural and remote India**.  **So, point III is correct.**
● **e-Gram Swaraj** is a comprehensive **digital platform** developed by the **Ministry of Panchayati Raj** **to** **support planning, budgeting, accounting, monitoring, and reporting in Gram Panchayats**. It is used by over 2.7 lakh Panchayati Raj Institutions across India and is central to digital governance at the grassroots level. The platform ensures transparency, accountability, and citizen access to Panchayat data. **So, point IV is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** The Ministry of Panchayati Raj (MoPR) is set to launch SabhaSaar, an Artificial Intelligence (AI)powered meeting summarization tool designed to automatically generate structured Minutes of Meeting (MoM) from Gram Sabha or other Panchayat meetings’ audio and video recordings.
